questions.question: What is a proposition? Differentiate between simple and compound propositions with examples.
keyPoints: Definition: Declarative statement that is either true or false
Simple proposition: Contains single statement (e.g., 'Delhi is capital of India')
Compound proposition: Combined using logical connectives (e.g., 'It is raining AND cold')

questions.question: Construct truth tables for: (i) p ∧ q (ii) p ∨ q (iii) p → q
keyPoints: p ∧ q true only when both p and q are true
p ∨ q false only when both p and q are false
p → q false only when p is true and q is false

questions.question: State and prove De Morgan's Laws for logical operators.
keyPoints: Proof by truth table showing both sides have identical truth values

questions.question: What are tautology, contradiction, and contingency? Give one example of each.
keyPoints: Tautology: Always true (e.g., p ∨ ¬p)
Contradiction: Always false (e.g., p ∧ ¬p)
Contingency: True or false depending on truth values (e.g., p → q)

questions.question: Explain universal and existential quantifiers with examples.
keyPoints: Universal (∀): 'For all' - ∀x P(x) true if every element satisfies
Existential (∃): 'There exists' - ∃x P(x) true if at least one element satisfies
Examples with specific domains like integers or real numbers

questions.question: Write the negations of: (i) ∀x P(x) (ii) ∃x P(x)
keyPoints: ¬∀x P(x) ≡ ∃x ¬P(x) - negation of 'for all' is 'there exists one that doesn't'
¬∃x P(x) ≡ ∀x ¬P(x) - negation of 'there exists' is 'none satisfy'
Important: quantifier changes when negating

questions.question: Explain Modus Ponens and Modus Tollens with one example each.
keyPoints: Modus Ponens: p → q, p ∴ q (affirming antecedent)
Modus Tollens: p → q, ¬q ∴ ¬p (denying consequent)
Examples: Modus Ponens - 'If it rains, ground wet; it rains; therefore ground wet'; Modus Tollens - 'If it rains, ground wet; ground not wet; therefore not raining'

questions.question: Discuss argument forms in propositional logic. Explain valid and invalid arguments with examples. How do you determine if an argument is valid?
keyPoints: Argument: premises followed by conclusion
Valid argument: conclusion must be true if premises are true
Test validity by truth table or logical manipulation
Examples of valid forms: Modus Ponens, Modus Tollens, Hypothetical Syllogism
Examples of invalid forms: Fallacy of affirming consequent, Fallacy of denying antecedent
Sound argument: valid + all premises true

questions.question: Explain predicate logic in detail. Discuss predicates, propositional functions, and types of quantifiers with examples. How does predicate logic extend propositional logic?
keyPoints: Predicate: function that becomes proposition when variables assigned values
Propositional function: P(x), P(x,y) - contains variables
Quantifiers bind variables: ∀ (universal), ∃ (existential)
Predicate logic can express 'All humans are mortal', 'Some birds cannot fly' which propositional logic cannot
Discuss scope of quantifiers, free vs bound variables
Extension: handles partial information, internal structure of propositions

questions.question: Explain the following with truth tables and examples: (i) Logical equivalence (ii) Conditional and biconditional statements (iii) Converse, inverse, and contrapositive
keyPoints: Logical equivalence: p ≡ q when p↔q is tautology
Conditional p→q: false only when T→F; equivalent to ¬p∨q
Biconditional p↔q: true when both have same truth value
Converse: q→p; Inverse: ¬p→¬q; Contrapositive: ¬q→¬p
Original and contrapositive equivalent; converse and inverse equivalent
Show with truth tables

questions.question: What are arguments in predicate logic? Explain universal instantiation, universal generalization, and rules for arguments with quantifiers with examples.
keyPoints: Predicate logic arguments use quantified statements
Universal Instantiation: from ∀x P(x), infer P(c) for specific c
Universal Generalization: from P(c) for arbitrary c, infer ∀x P(x)
Existential Instantiation: from ∃x P(x), infer P(c) for some c
Existential Generalization: from P(c) for specific c, infer ∃x P(x)
Show valid argument forms with quantified premises
